body: But in this case someone doesn't have to. Racists aren't a mindless force like climate change. They're humans making a choice.

subreddit.name: history
body: You're certainly entitled to your own experience, but I don't think it's a good argument for any universal reality. > They might have been exposed to poverty or disease just as much as we are now but the basic foundations of life had not been shaken and there was no impending feeling of doom like we have now with the crunch of climate change and all the discoveries we have made about "what is bad for us." This is the thing, though. People today are much less exposed to poverty or disease than in the 1950s/1960s. [Global life expectancy from 50 years ago]([URL] is much different [than it is today]([URL] You'll see that [rates of poverty have even declined]([URL] greatly over just the past 20 years,. too. In the 1950s there was the Soviet Union, the Chinese, and the bomb. The 60s and 70s had Vietnam, assassinations, invasions, and general unrest. The 80s was defined by drugs and AIDS and the 90s wasn't much better. Not to sound depressing, but fear and impending doom have been a common reality for your entire life. The society you live in has always had complicated problems, whether you payed close attention to them or not. It was like that even before the atomic bomb, too: people in America in 1929 thought the world was coming to its end much the same as people in 1860s America did. Maybe you're more aware now because your older and wiser, or maybe you have a point about technology making things seem more immediate. However, if history teaches us anything: its that doom is always just over the horizon, and humanity spares no effort in complicating things.
